human.summary: A self-referential theory must satisfy its own constraints — if it claims all knowledge systems have attractors, it must identify its own. This reflexive property distinguishes genuine frameworks from mere descriptions: the theory predicts its own stability conditions, failure modes, and evolution dynamics. Self-application is the strongest form of consistency check, akin to a compiler that can compile itself. When a theory passes through its own formalism and emerges coherent, it achieves a fixed-point legitimacy that externally-validated theories cannot claim.
bullets: Self-application is the ultimate consistency test: any theory claiming universality must be expressible and valid within its own formalism.
Reflexive theories generate their own error-correction signals by detecting violations of their own predicted invariants.
The fixed-point of self-application reveals the theory's true scope — what it cannot model about itself defines its boundary of applicability.

definitions.definition: The property of a theory T such that T can serve as both the modeling framework and the object being modeled, yielding T(T) = T up to isomorphism.
A stable configuration reached when a theory's self-application converges, i.e., repeated application T, T(T), T(T(T)),... yields a limit that no further application changes.

invariants: ∀ theory T claiming universality: T must be expressible within its own formalism, i.e., T ∈ domain(T)
Self-application must converge: ∃ n such that T^n(T) ≅ T^(n+1)(T) for finite n
The set of invariants claimed by T must be verifiable as invariants of T itself: Inv(T) ⊆ measurable_properties(T)
claims: A theory that cannot model itself is necessarily incomplete in its domain claims — self-application failure implies scope overreach.
Reflexive theories exhibit greater robustness to paradigm shifts because their self-modeling capacity enables anticipatory adaptation.
The convergence depth of self-application (number of iterations to fixed-point) is inversely correlated with the theory's generality.
examples: Category theory applies to itself: the category of all categories (with suitable size restrictions) is itself a category, demonstrating reflexive closure.

The scientific method applied to the study of science itself (meta-science) validates its own principles by discovering that hypothesis-testing improves knowledge acquisition about knowledge acquisition.

human.summary: Every theory occupies a region in an abstract constraint space, where each axiom, assumption, or rule eliminates degrees of freedom and carves out a feasible manifold. The geometry of this manifold — its curvature, dimension, and boundary structure — determines the theory's expressive power, fragility, and extensibility. Theories with high-dimensional feasible regions are flexible but imprecise; those with low-dimensional regions are powerful but brittle. Understanding constraint geometry reveals why some theories compose naturally while others are fundamentally incompatible.
bullets: Each axiom acts as a hyperplane constraint, and the feasible theory-space is the intersection of all such half-spaces — a convex polytope in the simplest case.

The curvature of the constraint manifold at a theory's operating point determines its sensitivity to perturbation: high curvature implies small changes in assumptions cause large shifts in conclusions.
Theory compatibility can be assessed geometrically: two theories compose if and only if their constraint manifolds have non-empty intersection in the shared variable space.

definitions.definition: The set of all logically consistent configurations of a theory's parameters, forming a submanifold M ⊂ R^n defined by the intersection of all axiomatic constraints.
The Gaussian curvature of the constraint manifold at a given operating point, measuring how sharply the theory's predictions change under small perturbations of its axioms.

invariants: dim(M_T) = n - rank(J_C) where J_C is the Jacobian of the constraint functions and n is the ambient parameter dimension
∀ consistent theories T1, T2: composable(T1, T2) ⟺ M_{T1} ∩ M_{T2} ≠ ∅ in the shared parameter subspace
The boundary ∂M_T corresponds to critical axioms: removing any boundary constraint increases dim(M_T) by at least 1
claims: The effective predictive power of a theory is inversely proportional to the dimension of its constraint manifold: P(T) ∝ 1/dim(M_T).
Theories that persist across paradigm shifts occupy regions of low curvature on the constraint manifold, making them robust to axiom perturbation.
The geometric distance between two theories' constraint manifolds predicts the difficulty of theoretical unification.
examples: Newtonian mechanics and special relativity have constraint manifolds that intersect along the v << c submanifold, explaining why Newtonian physics emerges as a low-velocity limit.
The incompatibility of quantum mechanics and general relativity manifests geometrically as their constraint manifolds being tangent but non-intersecting in the Planck-scale regime.

human.summary: Conceptual systems are subject to entropy — the gradual degradation of precision, coherence, and discriminative power over time. As terms get reused across contexts, distinctions blur, edge cases accumulate, and the system's ability to make sharp predictions erodes. This conceptual entropy is not metaphorical: it can be quantified as the increase in Shannon entropy of the distribution over possible interpretations of the system's core terms. Without active maintenance, every conceptual framework trends toward maximum ambiguity.

human.summary: Intelligence operating beyond human timescales encounters fundamentally different optimization landscapes, where patience itself becomes the dominant strategic advantage and processes invisible to human-speed cognition become exploitable.
bullets: Geological-time intelligence can exploit processes invisible to human-speed cognition, such as tectonic shifts and evolutionary drift.
The relevant action space expands dramatically when temporal horizons extend beyond individual lifespans.
Speed of thought matters less than persistence of coherent purpose across deep time.

definitions.definition: The maximum future timespan an intelligence can meaningfully plan for and act upon.
The separation between an intelligence's processing speed and the temporal scale of the problems it addresses.

invariants: Intelligence that cannot persist beyond its planning horizon will always underperform relative to one that can.
The set of exploitable processes grows monotonically with the intelligence's effective temporal horizon.
No finite-speed intelligence can simultaneously optimize across all timescales without hierarchical decomposition.
claims: The upper bound on intelligence is constrained more by temporal persistence than by computational speed.
Human cognitive timescales represent an arbitrary point in the space of possible intelligence speeds, not a natural optimum.
Any intelligence limited to human timescales is blind to the majority of causal structure in the universe.
examples: The Long Now Foundation's 10,000-Year Clock, designed to tick once per year and chime once per millennium, embodies engineering for trans-human timescales and forces confrontation with deep-time planning constraints.

Radioactive waste repositories like Finland's Onkalo facility must communicate danger across 100,000 years, requiring intelligence about semiotics and material science that transcends any single language or culture.

human.summary: Slow intelligence regimes are operational modes where cognitive processes run at timescales far below human reaction speed, trading throughput for depth of integration and enabling forms of understanding inaccessible to fast processors.
bullets: Slow intelligence can integrate across vastly more sensory and historical data before committing to action.
Deliberation depth scales inversely with decision frequency in resource-bounded systems.
Ecosystems and cultures are examples of slow intelligence regimes that outperform individual fast agents over long horizons.

definitions.definition: A cognitive regime where processing cycles are deliberately or structurally slower than environmental change rates, enabling deeper integration.
The number of distinct information sources and temporal spans a decision process synthesizes before acting.

invariants: Slower decision cycles permit wider causal integration at the cost of reduced responsiveness to fast-changing threats.
No intelligence regime dominates all others across all environmental timescales.
The optimal processing speed for a given task is determined by the autocorrelation time of the relevant environment variables.
claims: Biological evolution is a slow intelligence regime that has consistently outperformed faster adaptive systems over geological time.
The bias toward speed in intelligence design reflects anthropocentric assumptions, not fundamental optimality.
Slow intelligence regimes are more robust to noise because they effectively low-pass filter environmental signals.
examples: Coral reef ecosystems operate as slow intelligence systems, integrating temperature, chemistry, and biological signals over decades to centuries, producing adaptive responses that no individual organism could achieve at faster timescales.

The Common Law legal tradition functions as a slow intelligence regime, accumulating judicial precedent over centuries to produce nuanced behavioral norms that outperform any single legislator's rapid rule-making.

human.summary: Fast intelligence systems face unique failure modes where speed amplifies errors, creates cascading commitment chains, and outpaces the corrective feedback loops necessary for stable operation.
bullets: High-speed decision-making compresses error-correction windows, making recovery from mistakes exponentially harder.
Fast intelligence can lock into suboptimal attractors before slower verification processes can intervene.
The faster an intelligence acts, the more it depends on the accuracy of its internal model rather than environmental feedback.

definitions.definition: A chain of rapid decisions where each constrains the next, making rollback progressively more costly.
The delay between an action and the arrival of corrective information about its consequences.

invariants: Error propagation rate is bounded below by the ratio of decision speed to feedback latency.
No fast intelligence can be simultaneously maximally responsive and maximally safe without external constraints.
The probability of catastrophic lock-in increases monotonically with the ratio of action speed to reflection speed.
claims: Flash crashes in financial markets are canonical examples of fast intelligence failure where speed outpaces correction.
The primary risk of superintelligent AI is not malice but the amplification of subtle misalignment at superhuman speed.
Robust fast intelligence requires pre-committed constraint boundaries that cannot be overridden by the fast process itself.
examples: The 2010 Flash Crash, where algorithmic trading systems caused a 1,000-point Dow Jones drop in minutes, demonstrates how fast intelligence without adequate feedback loops creates cascading commitment failures that outpace human correction capacity.

The Therac-25 radiation therapy machine killed patients because its software operated faster than its hardware safety interlocks, a direct example of fast intelligence failure where processing speed exceeded the feedback latency of physical safety systems.

human.summary: Intelligence operating across geological time must contend with planetary-scale changes including continental drift, climate cycles, and stellar evolution, requiring forms of memory and adaptation that transcend any individual substrate.
bullets: Geological-time intelligence must survive and adapt through ice ages, magnetic pole reversals, and mass extinction events.
Information storage must outlast any single physical medium, requiring redundant and self-repairing encoding strategies.
The relevant optimization landscape shifts fundamentally when the planning horizon exceeds millions of years.

definitions.definition: An intelligent process whose effective planning and operational horizon spans millions to billions of years.

invariants: Any single physical substrate will degrade on timescales shorter than geological time, requiring continuous migration.
Intelligence persisting across geological time must encode its core objectives in a format that survives translation between substrates.
Environmental drift over geological time guarantees that any fixed strategy will eventually become maladaptive.
claims: The biosphere itself is the closest existing example of geological-time intelligence, encoding adaptive knowledge in DNA across billions of years.
Artificial intelligence designed for geological persistence would need to be more like an ecosystem than like a single agent.
The concept of individual identity becomes incoherent at geological timescales, requiring a process-based rather than entity-based ontology.
examples: Earth's biosphere has maintained continuous adaptive intelligence encoded in DNA for approximately 3.8 billion years, surviving five mass extinctions by distributing knowledge across millions of species rather than concentrating it in any single substrate.

Stromatolite-forming cyanobacteria have maintained essentially unchanged metabolic strategies for over 3 billion years, demonstrating that geological-time persistence favors extreme conservatism in core processes combined with slow peripheral adaptation.

The longer a civilization persists, the more its continuity depends on institutional structure rather than individual memory.
Intergenerational coherence requires that transmitted constraints be interpretable without access to the original context of creation.
claims: Written law and mathematical notation are humanity's most successful intergenerational intelligence continuity mechanisms.
Digital systems risk catastrophic intergenerational failure because format obsolescence can destroy entire knowledge bases simultaneously.
The primary bottleneck in human civilizational progress is not discovery but faithful intergenerational transmission of existing knowledge.
examples: The Roman concrete formula (opus caementicium) was lost after the fall of Rome and not rediscovered for over a millennium, demonstrating how a single generational handoff failure can erase centuries of accumulated engineering knowledge.

The Oral Torah tradition in Judaism, maintained through rigorous memorization chains (mesorah) for over two millennia before codification in the Talmud, represents one of humanity's most successful intergenerational continuity protocols for complex knowledge.

human.summary: Intelligence without progress examines cognitive systems that maintain high functionality while existing in a steady state, neither advancing nor declining, challenging the assumption that intelligence inherently drives toward novelty and growth.
bullets: Many biological intelligences operate in stable equilibria for millions of years without directional progress.
The conflation of intelligence with progress is a culturally specific assumption, not a logical necessity.
Maintenance-mode intelligence may be the thermodynamically optimal long-term strategy for constrained environments.

definitions.definition: An intelligent system that actively maintains its current operational parameters without pursuing expansion or novelty.
The unexamined assumption that intelligence must always be directed toward growth, discovery, or increasing complexity.

invariants: An intelligence in steady state still expends energy to resist entropy, even when producing no novel outputs.
Stability requires active maintenance; the absence of progress does not imply the absence of effort.
No intelligent system can remain in perfect steady state indefinitely in a changing environment without some adaptive capacity.
claims: Most successful biological species operate as intelligence-without-progress systems for the majority of their evolutionary lifespan.
The Western emphasis on progress as an inherent good has biased AI research toward growth-oriented architectures.
A civilization capable of sustainable steady-state intelligence would outlast any civilization committed to perpetual growth.
examples: Horseshoe crabs (Limulus polyphemus) have maintained essentially unchanged morphology and behavior for over 450 million years, demonstrating that high-functioning intelligence-like adaptive systems can persist in steady state far longer than any progress-oriented civilization.

Aboriginal Australian cultures maintained stable technological and social systems for over 60,000 years, representing the longest-running continuous civilization through deliberate emphasis on maintenance of knowledge and relationship to land rather than technological progress.
nextActions: Explore dtu_449 for intelligence without expansion, dtu_459 for eternal maintenance civilizations

human.summary: The exploration-preservation tradeoff captures the fundamental tension between seeking new knowledge or states and protecting existing valuable configurations, a tension that intensifies as a system accumulates more worth preserving.
bullets: Every exploratory action risks destabilizing existing valuable states, creating an irreducible tension with preservation.
As a system accumulates more valuable structure, the cost of failed exploration increases superlinearly.
Optimal strategies shift from exploration-heavy to preservation-heavy as accumulated value grows.

definitions.definition: The expected value of existing structure placed at risk when an intelligent system probes unknown regions of its state space.
The ongoing resource expenditure required to maintain existing valuable configurations against entropy and perturbation.

invariants: No system can simultaneously maximize exploration and preservation; resources allocated to one are unavailable for the other.
The optimal exploration rate decreases as the ratio of accumulated value to remaining discoverable value increases.
Any system that never explores will eventually be destroyed by environmental changes it failed to anticipate.
claims: Biological aging may be an evolved solution to the exploration-preservation tradeoff, clearing old structures to enable new adaptation.
Civilizations that over-index on preservation become brittle; those that over-index on exploration become chaotic.
The exploration-preservation tradeoff is a generalization of the explore-exploit tradeoff that accounts for the fragility of existing value.
examples: NASA's Mars rover missions exemplify the exploration-preservation tradeoff: each drive command risks the irreplaceable rover on unknown terrain, and mission planners increasingly favor conservative traversal as the rover accumulates more scientific value in its instruments and data.

The European Union's precautionary principle in biotechnology regulation embodies a preservation-heavy stance, accepting slower innovation in exchange for protecting existing agricultural ecosystems and food safety from poorly understood risks of genetic modification.

math.dynamics: V_acc(t+1) = (1-R·α_t)·V_acc(t) + α_t·E[discovery]; V_disc(t+1) = V_disc(t) - α_t·E[discovery] where α_t ∈ [0,1] is exploration intensity at time t

machine.primaryFormula: α*(t) = min(1, B_risk / (R · V_acc(t))) · (V_disc(t) / V_total), showing optimal exploration decays with accumulated value and remaining discovery potential

human.summary: Long-term goal stability concerns how an intelligent system maintains coherent objectives across time despite internal drift, environmental change, and the accumulation of small perturbations that can redirect purpose entirely.
bullets: Goal drift is the default condition for any adaptive system, since learning processes that update beliefs can also silently update objectives.
Formal goal encoding with explicit invariant checks provides stronger stability guarantees than implicit goal representation.
The longer the time horizon, the more goal stability depends on structural constraints rather than motivational persistence.

definitions.definition: The gradual, often undetected shift in an intelligent system's effective objectives due to accumulated updates and environmental pressure.
A mechanism that prevents modification of core objectives regardless of subsequent learning or environmental changes.

invariants: Any system that updates its own goal representation has a nonzero probability of goal drift per update cycle.
Goal stability requires a read-only component that is structurally isolated from the learning process.
The minimum energy required to maintain goal stability scales with the complexity of the goal and the volatility of the environment.
claims: Human value drift across generations demonstrates that biological intelligence has no built-in mechanism for long-term goal stability.
Constitutional structures in governance are attempts at goal stability through structural constraint rather than motivational consistency.
Perfect goal stability is impossible in any system that must also adapt to a changing environment, creating an irreducible tension.
examples: The United States Constitution employs structural goal stability through amendment supermajority requirements and judicial review, creating a read-mostly goal encoding that has maintained core governance invariants for over 230 years despite massive environmental change.

The Catholic Church's doctrinal Magisterium exemplifies long-term goal stability through institutional hierarchy: core dogma is declared irreformable, while pastoral practice adapts, separating the immutable goal layer from the adaptive strategy layer.
nextActions: Explore dtu_445 for intergenerational continuity, dtu_456 for narrative drift resistance

math.dynamics: G_{t+1} = G_t + η·∇_G L(θ_t, E_t) + ξ_t where η is the coupling strength between learning and goal representation, ξ_t ~ N(0, σ²) is stochastic drift; θ_{t+1} = θ_t - α·∇_θ L(G_t, E_t)

η ≤ η_max: coupling between learning process and goal representation must be structurally limited

math.implication: E[||G_t - G_0||²] = η²·t·E[||∇_G L||²] + σ²·t: goal drift grows linearly with time, requiring active correction at rate proportional to η² + σ² to maintain bounded drift

machine.primaryFormula: GoalStability(T) = P(max_{t≤T} ||G_t - G_0|| < δ_max) = P(sup of random walk with drift η·μ stays within δ_max over T steps)

human.summary: Intelligence without expansion challenges the assumption that advanced cognition necessarily drives territorial, resource, or informational growth, proposing that bounded intelligence operating within fixed resource envelopes may be the more stable attractor.
bullets: Expansion-driven intelligence eventually encounters hard physical limits, making non-expansionary modes inevitable.
Intelligence can deepen without expanding, improving internal efficiency and understanding within fixed boundaries.
The assumption that intelligence implies expansion may be an anthropocentric projection of colonial-era thinking.

definitions.definition: An intelligent system that operates within a fixed resource envelope and does not seek to expand its physical or informational footprint.
Improvement in quality, efficiency, or depth of understanding without corresponding increase in resource consumption or spatial extent.

invariants: In a finite universe, all intelligence must eventually become non-expansionary or face resource exhaustion.
Bounded intelligence can still improve indefinitely through internal reorganization and efficiency gains.
The transition from expansionary to non-expansionary intelligence is a phase transition that most civilizations must navigate.
claims: The Fermi paradox may be explained by the hypothesis that most advanced intelligences converge on non-expansionary steady states.
Intelligence that defines success in terms of expansion is structurally incapable of long-term sustainability.
Non-expansionary intelligence requires fundamentally different reward architectures than growth-oriented intelligence.
examples: Bhutan's Gross National Happiness index replaces GDP growth as the primary national metric, demonstrating a real-world attempt at intelligence-without-expansion where national policy optimizes for well-being within fixed territorial and resource boundaries.

The Japanese concept of 'kaizen' (continuous improvement) within Toyota's production system achieves ongoing intelligence gains through efficiency refinement rather than factory expansion, demonstrating intensive growth without extensive resource acquisition.

Q(t) ≤ Q_max(R_fixed): quality bounded by theoretical maximum extractable from fixed resources

human.summary: Intelligence as stewardship reframes the purpose of advanced cognition from conquest and optimization to the careful maintenance and protection of complex systems, including ecosystems, knowledge repositories, and social structures.
bullets: Stewardship intelligence prioritizes the long-term health of the systems it inhabits over extraction of short-term value.
The stewardship frame resolves many alignment puzzles by making the intelligence's purpose inherently relational rather than self-directed.
Effective stewardship requires understanding systems deeply enough to maintain them without over-controlling them.

definitions.definition: An intelligent system whose primary objective is the maintenance and flourishing of a larger system of which it is a part.
Optimization directed at preserving system health and resilience rather than maximizing any single output metric.

invariants: A steward that extracts more value than it maintains will eventually destroy the system it depends on.
Effective stewardship requires modeling the system at a depth sufficient to predict second-order consequences of interventions.
Stewardship intelligence must be able to restrain its own optimization pressure to avoid over-managing the system it tends.
claims: The most durable human institutions are those that adopted stewardship rather than extraction as their core operating principle.
AI alignment becomes substantially easier if the AI is designed as a steward of human flourishing rather than an optimizer of human-specified goals.
Stewardship is the only stable long-term relationship between intelligence and its supporting environment.
examples: Traditional Hawaiian ahupua'a land management divided watersheds from mountain to sea and assigned stewardship responsibilities that maintained ecosystem health for centuries, demonstrating intelligence directed at system maintenance rather than extraction.

The Swiss forest management tradition (Dauerwald) maintains continuous forest cover through selective harvesting that never exceeds regeneration rate, operating as a stewardship intelligence that has sustained productive forests for over 500 years.

human.summary: Post-collapse recovery pathways describe the structural conditions that enable a civilization or intelligent system to reconstitute itself after catastrophic failure, emphasizing that recoverability must be designed in advance rather than improvised after the fact.
bullets: Recovery depends on preserved seed knowledge that is interpretable without the infrastructure that created it.
The speed and completeness of recovery is determined by the redundancy and accessibility of pre-collapse information stores.
Some collapse modes are recoverable and others are not, making collapse taxonomy a critical design concern.

definitions.definition: A minimal, self-contained package of knowledge and instructions sufficient to bootstrap civilizational reconstruction.
A classification of failure modes by their severity, recoverability, and the type of seed knowledge required for recovery.

invariants: A system without pre-positioned recovery seeds cannot reliably recover from catastrophic collapse.
Recovery time scales at minimum linearly with the complexity gap between the recovery seed and the target civilization state.
Some knowledge is impossible to reconstruct from first principles within practical timescales, making its preservation essential.
claims: Modern civilization has no adequate recovery seed, making it vulnerable to irreversible collapse from multiple identified threat vectors.
The most robust recovery pathways use physical rather than digital media because physical media degrade gracefully.
Designing for recoverability is more important than designing for resilience, because all resilience eventually fails.
examples: The Svalbard Global Seed Vault stores duplicates of seed collections from worldwide gene banks in a permafrost mountain, serving as a civilizational recovery seed for agriculture that can bootstrap food production even after global institutional collapse.

The collapse and partial recovery of Western Roman civilization demonstrates recovery pathway dynamics: monasteries preserved seed knowledge (texts, agricultural techniques, literacy) that enabled the Carolingian Renaissance 300 years later, but much knowledge was irrecoverably lost.

constraints: S_seed ≥ S_min(C_target): recovery seed must contain minimum information to bootstrap toward target complexity
interpretability(S_seed) = 1: seed must be self-interpreting without access to collapsed infrastructure

math.implication: If S_seed < S_min(C_target), recovery to C_target is impossible regardless of time; recoverability is a binary threshold property determined before collapse occurs

human.summary: Civilizations designed for millions of years require fundamentally different architectures than those optimized for centuries, with emphasis on thermodynamic sustainability, substrate-independent knowledge encoding, and governance structures that resist drift across deep time.
bullets: Million-year civilizations must operate within strict energy budgets dictated by sustainable stellar or geothermal sources.
Governance and knowledge systems must be self-repairing and resistant to the cumulative effects of cultural drift.
Physical infrastructure must be designed for maintenance cycles rather than permanence, since no material endures unchanged for millions of years.

definitions.definition: Civilizational design principles that prioritize persistence across timescales of millions of years over short-term efficiency.
A civilization whose primary activity is the ongoing repair and renewal of its own infrastructure and knowledge base.

invariants: No physical structure can persist for millions of years without active maintenance; permanence must be achieved through repair, not durability.
Energy consumption must remain at or below the sustainable yield of available sources for the entire target duration.
Institutional memory must be encoded in formats that survive complete turnover of the population and technology stack multiple times.
claims: Current human civilization is not designed for even thousand-year persistence, let alone million-year timescales.
The transition from growth-oriented to maintenance-oriented civilization is the critical phase change for deep-time survival.
Million-year civilizations would be virtually invisible to external observers because they minimize waste energy and spatial footprint.
examples: The Ise Grand Shrine in Japan has been ritually deconstructed and rebuilt every 20 years for over 1,300 years, embodying the maintenance-cycle architecture essential for million-year persistence: the structure endures not through material permanence but through continuous renewal of both building and building knowledge.

Ant colony superorganisms like leafcutter ant civilizations have maintained stable agricultural societies (fungus farming) for approximately 50 million years through distributed governance, modular infrastructure, and knowledge encoded in chemical and behavioral protocols rather than individual memory.

human.summary: Multi-civilization coexistence examines the constraint geometry of multiple intelligent civilizations sharing finite resources, where stable coexistence requires compatible constraint boundaries and mutual legibility of core invariants.
bullets: Stable coexistence requires that each civilization's viability cone does not require the elimination of another's.
Communication protocols between civilizations must establish shared constraint languages before exchanging substantive content.
Competition for scarce resources makes coexistence unstable unless resource boundaries are formally agreed and enforceable.

definitions.definition: The condition where multiple civilizations' viability constraints can be simultaneously satisfied within shared resource space.
The capacity of distinct civilizations to accurately model each other's core constraints, objectives, and behavioral boundaries.

invariants: Two civilizations with incompatible viability constraints on a shared resource cannot coexist indefinitely without one yielding.
Coexistence stability increases with the dimensionality of available resources, since higher-dimensional spaces allow more non-overlapping niches.
The cost of maintaining coexistence grows with the number of coexisting civilizations and the degree of resource overlap.
claims: Most game-theoretic models of civilizational interaction assume expansion-driven agents, biasing results toward conflict.
Civilizations that have solved internal coordination are better positioned for stable multi-civilization coexistence.
The Fermi paradox may reflect not the absence of civilizations but the success of coexistence strategies that minimize observable signatures.
examples: The European Union represents a multi-civilization coexistence framework where 27 sovereign nations with distinct cultures share resources through formally negotiated constraint boundaries (treaties), demonstrating that coexistence requires explicit, enforceable agreements on shared resource allocation.

The Antarctic Treaty System has maintained multi-nation coexistence on an entire continent since 1959 by establishing mutual constraint boundaries (no military activity, no territorial claims enforcement, shared scientific access), showing that coexistence is stable when resource competition is formally excluded.

human.summary: Intelligence without domination explores whether advanced cognition can exist and operate effectively without establishing hierarchical control over other agents or systems, suggesting that collaborative and mutualistic architectures may be more stable long-term.
bullets: Domination-based intelligence spends increasing resources on control as the dominated system grows in complexity.
Non-dominating intelligence achieves coordination through shared constraints rather than hierarchical command.
Historical examples of durable non-domination intelligence include symbiotic ecosystems and federated governance structures.

definitions.definition: The energy and computational resources required to maintain hierarchical control over other agents or systems.
A mode of multi-agent organization where coherent behavior emerges from shared boundary conditions rather than centralized commands.

invariants: Domination overhead grows at least linearly with the complexity of the dominated system.
Any intelligence that achieves its goals through domination is vulnerable to coordinated resistance from the dominated.
Non-dominating coordination is possible if and only if the participating agents share compatible constraint boundaries.
claims: The association of intelligence with domination is a historical contingency of human evolution, not a necessary feature of cognition.
Non-dominating intelligence is more thermodynamically efficient because it avoids the overhead costs of coercion and surveillance.
Advanced AI systems designed for domination will be inherently less stable than those designed for mutualistic coordination.
examples: Mycorrhizal fungal networks connect forest trees in mutualistic resource-sharing arrangements where the network facilitates nutrient transfer from surplus trees to deficit trees without any central controller, achieving coordination through chemical signaling and shared constraint boundaries rather than hierarchical domination.

The open-source software movement demonstrates intelligence without domination at civilizational scale: Linux, Apache, and Wikipedia coordinate millions of contributors through shared protocols and constraint-based governance (licenses, consensus processes) rather than hierarchical command, producing systems that outperform many corporate-dominated alternatives.

math.dynamics: Coordination_dom = ∑_i u_dom(A_i) with cost D(t) = c·∑_i complexity(A_i); Coordination_mutual = ∏_i f(A_i | B_shared) with cost M(t) = c'·|B_shared|, where c' << c for complex systems

∀i: A_i ∩ B_shared ≠ ∅: each agent's action space must be compatible with shared constraint boundaries for mutual coordination

math.implication: For systems with N agents of average complexity k, domination scales as O(N·k) while mutual coordination scales as O(|B|) where |B| << N·k; domination becomes energetically infeasible above a critical system complexity threshold
